title: This World Cup, Bigger Might Not Really Be Better
gist: The 2026 World Cup's three-country, 48-team scale is straining economics and logistics: per-fan costs north of $5,000, soft hotel bookings, near-$1B in public security grants, and a structural shift where only the richest nations can host alone.

insights.claim: Average attendance cost in US host cities is running north of $5,000 per person before flights between venues, with expected total spend around $5,400, versus the $720-$2,500 that visitors to Qatar 2022 spent.
insights.speaker: Chris Hamill-Stewart
insights.so_what: A 2-7x jump in per-fan cost over the prior edition signals pricing-out risk; watch host-city hospitality and travel demand for downside surprises.
insights.quote: Average attendance cost in the US host cities is running north of $5,000 a head, before you’ve factored in flights between venues

insights.claim: US hotels are already reporting World Cup bookings below expectations because high inter-city flight costs are suppressing demand, showing that tournament scale does not guarantee attendance.
insights.speaker: Chris Hamill-Stewart
insights.so_what: Bigger event does not equal bigger crowds; a caution flag for hotel REITs and host-city tourism revenue projections.
insights.quote: US hotels are already reporting bookings below expectations. Scale doesn’t guarantee the crowds will show up.
insights.locator: The need to book flights, not trains or taxis

insights.claim: Because of the vast distances across 16 cities and four time zones, air travel is effectively the only option for fans and teams, and airlines have been adding flights to capture World Cup travelers.
insights.speaker: Chris Hamill-Stewart
insights.so_what: Forced air dependence shifts spend from local transit to airlines; a potential demand pocket for North American carriers during the tournament window.
insights.quote: the only option for fans and teams is flights, which airlines have been adding to accommodate potential World Cup travelers
insights.locator: This year, because of the vast distances

insights.claim: Total public security grants in the US and Canada approach nearly $1 billion ($625M US federal grants to host cities, $200M+ DHS anti-drone grants, ~$104M Canadian grants), likely just a fraction of the real cost of securing the tournament.
insights.speaker: Chris Hamill-Stewart
insights.so_what: Concrete sizing of a public-funded security spend wave; anti-drone, surveillance, and physical-security vendors are direct beneficiaries.
insights.quote: That brings total public grants in Canada and the US alone to nearly $1 billion—likely just a fraction of the real costs of securing the tournament.

insights.claim: The US has earmarked over $200 million in DHS anti-drone grants because hostile actors increasingly have access to drones, making counter-UAS a named, funded priority for the event.
insights.speaker: Chris Hamill-Stewart
insights.so_what: Counter-drone tech is moving from niche to mandated event spend; track counter-UAS suppliers for tournament-linked contracts.
insights.quote: the Department of Homeland Security has made over $200 million worth of grants available to states to buy anti-drone technology
insights.locator: The US federal government has issued $625 million

insights.claim: The core 2026 security challenge is interoperability across multiple cities, jurisdictions, agencies, and technology ecosystems in three countries, not just the number of systems involved.
insights.speaker: Chris Hamill-Stewart
insights.so_what: Standardization bodies (e.g., Onvif) and integration vendors gain relevance as fragmented physical-security systems must exchange data.
insights.quote: The challenge is not simply the number of systems involved, but whether those systems can exchange information efficiently

themes.theme: FIFA's growth strategy and competitive moat

insights.claim: FIFA is pursuing growth-at-all-costs (48 teams, 6 billion forecast engagements vs 5 billion prior) to defend soccer's market share against the NBA, NFL, and Formula One expanding globally.
insights.speaker: Chris Hamill-Stewart
insights.so_what: Frames the World Cup expansion as a defensive market-share play; relevant for media-rights and sponsorship valuation across rival leagues.
insights.quote: to ensure that football remains robust, relevant, prominent and that it doesn’t begin losing market share—to the NBA... to the NFL... and to Formula One
insights.locator: The Future of the World Cup

insights.claim: The tournament has grown too large for most nations to host alone, so future hosts will be limited to the enormously rich (China, Saudi Arabia), those willing to build from scratch (India), or multi-country shared bids.
insights.speaker: Chris Hamill-Stewart
insights.so_what: Host eligibility consolidating toward wealthy/state-backed bidders concentrates future infrastructure and sponsorship flows in those geographies.
insights.quote: The days of a single traditional football nation hosting alone are probably numbered.
